macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview of Nezikin Collection from Best Land (Meitav Sadeihu)

micro_segments.title: Opening: Omer count and daf introduction
Review of previous discussion: Meitav Sadeihu debate between Rabbi Akiva and Rabbi Yishmael
Core question: Subjective best (Bishlo) vs. objective standard (Beshel Olam)
Gemara's immediate answer: Meitav Sadeihu clearly means his best land

macro_segments.title: Three Categories of Creditors and Land Quality Distribution

micro_segments.title: Braitah: Three types of collection and their corresponding land grades
Nezikin collects from Idis (Grade A), Baal Chov from Beinonis (Grade B), Kesuba from Ziboris (Grade C)
Rationale: Loans are hard to encourage; women's marriage incentives
Braitah scenarios: All Grade A; all Grade B; all Grade C only
Mixed grades: A+B, B+C, A+C scenarios and distribution rules

macro_segments.title: Contradiction between Braitot and the Subjective Standard Explanation

micro_segments.title: Problem: If standards are subjective, Grade B becomes Grade A, so Baal Chov should be downgraded
First resolution: Lien-based explanation—debt incurred when A existed protects B status
Two contradictory Braitot identified and visualized
Second resolution: Both braitot assume subjective standard, differ on whether A was ever present
Third resolution: Braitot debate objective (Beshel Olam) vs. subjective (Bishlo) standards

macro_segments.title: Ula's Teaching on Baal Chov's Rights and the Rabbinic Takana

micro_segments.title: Ravina's approach: Braitot reflect debate over Ula's teaching on Torah law vs. rabbinic enactment
Ula: Torah law limits Baal Chov to Ziboris (Grade C) based on initiative principle
Torah source: 'Betzatzach Tamod'—collateral brought at creditor's initiative
Tosafot challenge: Collateral case differs from land payment; alternative resolution offered
Takana explanation: Rabbis upgrade Baal Chov to Beinonis to encourage lending (Ne'ilat Delet)
Dispute whether Takana applies always or only when choice exists

macro_segments.title: Machar Lokeach: Sales of Property and Creditor Collection Rights

micro_segments.title: Braitah: Selling property to one or three buyers on same day vs. different days
Same-day sales: Buyers stand in seller's place; creditors collect according to grade
Different-day sales: All creditors must collect from last buyer (Kulam Govin Min Ha'Acharon)
Principle: Seller's property is primary responsibility; buyers are fallback
Each prior buyer can redirect creditors to subsequent buyer's property

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis of Single vs. Multiple Buyer Cases

micro_segments.title: Single buyer on same day is obvious; real case is sequential purchases
Sequential purchases: All creditors forced to collect from last buyer
Potential objection: Why can't creditors collect from earlier grades if last purchase is Grade C?
Response: Buyer's leverage—threat to return property or withhold agreement
Problem with leverage: Doesn't work against orphans; threatens orderly distribution

macro_segments.title: The Takana of Protecting Purchasers and Its Waiver

micro_segments.title: Rabbis enacted takana: No collection from liened property when free property exists
Purpose: Protect purchasers from creditor harassment
Buyer can waive takana if it works against his interest
Principle: Rights made to protect can be waived by beneficiary
Example: Woman can waive support-labor exchange if she earns high income

macro_segments.title: Application: Buyer Sells B and C, Retains A

micro_segments.title: Case: Michael bought A, B, C from original owner; sells B, C to Jonathan; keeps A
Result: All creditors collect from Michael's retained Grade A
Reason: A is last property; creditors fundamentally had rights there

macro_segments.title: Application: Buyer Sells A, Retains B and C

micro_segments.title: Case: Michael bought A, B, C; sells A to Jonathan; keeps B, C
Problem: Creditors all had rights on A; if A is gone, do they all get A from Jonathan?
Abaye's position: All creditors collect from Jonathan's A
Rava's position: Michael could have waived takana and distributed A, B, C among creditors
Rava's reasoning: Sale transfers not just property but waiver power; creditors revert to Michael
Conclusion: Jonathan receives A with only Nezikin lien; Baal Chov and Kesuba return to Michael for B and C

topical_tags.term: Ne'ilat Delet (Not Closing Door to Borrowers)
Ula (Amora who taught Torah law limits Baal Chov)
Kulam Govin Min Ha'Acharon (All Collect from Last)
